title: Quiz: Ion Trapping Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of Paul traps, Mathieu stability, and ion confinement physics

questions.question: According to Earnshaw's theorem, why can't static electric fields alone trap a charged particle?

options.text: Laplace's equation prevents a potential minimum in all three directions
The particle would be heated by the field

questions.explanation: Laplace's equation (nabla^2 Phi = 0) requires that the sum of second derivatives equals zero. This means if the potential curves upward in two directions, it must curve downward in the third - making stable 3D trapping impossible.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a Paul trap and a Penning trap?

options.text: Paul traps use RF electric fields; Penning traps use static magnetic + electric fields
Paul traps work at room temperature; Penning traps require cryogenic cooling
Paul traps can only hold one ion; Penning traps hold many
Paul traps are larger than Penning traps

questions.explanation: Paul traps achieve confinement using time-varying (RF) electric fields, while Penning traps use a combination of static magnetic field (for radial confinement) and static electric field (for axial confinement).

questions.question: In a Paul trap, what is 'secular motion'?

options.text: Motion at the RF drive frequency
Slow harmonic oscillation in the time-averaged pseudopotential

questions.explanation: Secular motion is the slow harmonic oscillation at frequency omega_s << Omega (the RF frequency). It represents motion in the effective time-averaged 'pseudopotential' created by the RF fields.

questions.question: Micromotion in a Paul trap is always beneficial because it provides additional confinement.
questions.explanation: False! While some micromotion is inherent to Paul trap operation, excess micromotion (caused by stray fields pushing the ion off the RF null) is harmful. It causes heating and Doppler shifts that degrade quantum operations.

questions.question: The Mathieu equation describes ion motion in a Paul trap. For stable trapping in the primary stability region, the parameter q should satisfy:
questions.explanation: The primary stability region of the Mathieu equation requires q < 0.908 (approximately). Most ion traps operate with q ~ 0.2-0.4 for optimal stability and minimal micromotion.

questions.question: Which ion species is commonly used for hyperfine qubits in trapped ion quantum computers?
questions.explanation: Yb-171 is a popular choice for hyperfine qubits because it has a nuclear spin of 1/2, creating well-defined hyperfine ground states with ~12.6 GHz splitting. Ca-40 and Sr-88 are used for optical qubits (no hyperfine structure).

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of trapped ion qubits? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Very long coherence times (minutes to hours)
Easy fabrication with standard CMOS processes

questions.explanation: Trapped ions offer exceptional coherence (a), the highest demonstrated gate fidelities (b), and natural all-to-all connectivity through shared motional modes (c). However, they require complex laser systems and vacuum, not standard CMOS fabrication (d).

questions.question: In a linear Paul trap with multiple ions, how do the ions arrange themselves?

options.text: In a linear chain along the trap axis (Coulomb crystal)

questions.explanation: Multiple ions in a linear Paul trap form a Coulomb crystal - a stable linear chain along the trap axis where Coulomb repulsion between ions is balanced by the confining potential. This arrangement is essential for many-qubit operations.
